content:
When we are creating the shape of our essays, the outlining phase is the perfect place to play around with how you want your argument's narrative to unfold. It is looking at your essay from a macro (zoomed out) level as opposed to a micro (zoomed in, sentence by sentence, paragraph by paragraph) level; while zoomed out, you can get a clearer sense of whether or not the order you have chosen will serve your specific argument or narrative best. We want to try and avoid the ‘and then’ syndrome, which goes something like this: “This happened, and then this happened, and then this happened, and then finally this happened.” Instead, the goal should be more along the lines of something like this: “This happened, which lead to this happening, and because of that happening this happened, all finally coming together for the final happening.”

Think of your introductory paragraph as the way to hook the reader. In terms of shape, imagine it as an inverted pyramid (or the gas tank in a car); you start broad with the main crux of what you plan on talking about, become more defined as you introduce the reader to the essential elements of the topic, and finally at your narrowest point you compose your well-defined and concise thesis.
context: From document: Week 1 Retention Tracking — chunk 3 (chunk 3/133)
